text: This is an interesting case of a young male who's had four previous inguinal hernia repairs. He had two open inguinal hernias and then he ended up having a robotic right inguinal hernia with a barred three max mesh that you can start to see into view there. So he came to me for chronic abdominal pain. He had an open plug-in patch initially. Then he had a triple neurectomy, mesh plug removal, followed by an Anle-Lichtenstein repair. That recurred. Then he had a robotic inguinal hernia repair with a barred three-D max mesh. The guy was complaining of pubic pain after his robotic inguinal hernia. He's having significant pain after the robotic inguinal hernia in both the right groin, but also mainly in the suprapubic region. So I took him back to the operating room. These were all done at, you know, St. Elsewhere. Came to me for chronic pubic pain.

The surgeon grasps the Bard 3D Max mesh, entering the deep space above the transversalis fascia. The mesh is manipulated within the superficial plane, avoiding the infraepigastric vessels, and is pushed down while maintaining its integrity as a single unit.

The surgeon carefully dissects through the mesh, maintaining a position right above it while navigating around the vessels. The scissors are used to cut through the mesh, revealing a deep flap above and muscle below. The surgeon sweeps tissue off the mesh, working medially with gentle traction to visualize the plane.

The surgeon crosses over the surgical field, encountering suture material and metal tacks embedded in the mesh. As the dissection progresses laterally, nerve tissue becomes visible amidst debris, including mesh and suture material. The surgeon splits the mesh and approaches the pubic tubercle, seeking anatomical landmarks while observing mesh material and metal tacks.

The surgeon uses a marking pen to identify areas of pain on the patient's abdomen, focusing on the mesh visible from the left side and the right side. The surgeon carefully splits the mesh near the pubic bone, revealing metal tacks. The assistant applies pressure on marked spots, highlighting areas of pain. The surgeon begins unscrewing and removing the metal tacks, which are located near the pubic bone.

The surgeon meticulously removes metal tacks and clips from the area beneath the pubic bone, splitting the mesh and peeling off material from the raw surface. The iliac vessels appear distorted, prompting careful medial dissection as they are pushed laterally due to mesh placement.
The surgeon encounters bleeding after removing a clip from a vessel, possibly related to a previous varicocele repair. The bleeding is controlled by suturing the vessel closed.

The surgeon carefully sweeps and pushes the mesh down, exposing the chord structures underneath. Clips are visible laterally, along with suture material. The surgeon removes a clip, revealing a vein, which is then sutured closed to stop the bleeding. The iliac and spermatic cord are positioned away from the surgical field.

The surgeon continues working laterally at the deep internal ring, where two clips are visible on the ilioinguinal nerve. The clips are carefully removed from the nerve, which was previously mistaken for a blood vessel. The surgeon proceeds with dissection from medial to lateral, achieving a release of tissue.

The surgeon identifies partially dissolved old vikral suture material while working on top of the spermatic cord. The mesh is peeled down using gentle cautery, sweeping fat downwards. The iliac vein is visible to the right, with the mesh stuck on it. The surgeon continues lateral dissection to deepen the pocket for mesh reapplication, sweeping tissue off the iliac vein.

The surgeon places an extra-large three max mesh into the surgical site, extending the pocket laterally to accommodate it. The mesh is carefully peeled off from areas where it was previously fixated, including regions marked by clips associated with high pain intensity. The surgeon cleans the muscle, removing the remaining mesh, ensuring a smooth fit.

I saw the guy at one week, and then I saw him again at three weeks, and then three months. At one week, it was difficult to tell if he still had pain or not. Everything was still kind of sore, but he said it was described a different type of pain. At three weeks, the burning sensation was completely gone on his right lateral side. And then at three months, all of his pain was gone, and he was back to work. And according to him, he was a new man.
